segments.text: everybody. Thanks for the opportunity to be here.
So my name's Stu Hamilton. I'm the Chief Executive of Mercury, and I'm supported
today with Christina Healey. I can see Christina there.
Christina is our Principal Policy and Planning Advisor.
So I'll talk for about, 10 minutes or so, and then leave plenty of time for
questions. The more technical questions, Christina will answer, and I'll, I'll
stick with the higher level strategic questions.
So we're, we're here to speak to our, our submission.
Uh, we also have sup- support the, the detailed submission from the
Electricity Sector Environment Group, which I believe, you've seen.
It contains rationale and potential solutions, and guide rails we'll think will
help.
My framing today is pretty simple.
And New Zealand needs to build and maintain renewable electricity infrastructure at
scale. And we need to do that in a way that keeps operating reliably because
that underpins electrification, that underpins emissions reduction and
economic growth. These bills take a funnel approach, and that
means to us that the biggest gains come from getting the upstream settings
right. Those are the national instruments and the regional combined plans,
so that the downstream and consenting and permitting is far more predictable
and less adversarial. We're conscious that dur-
durable reform has to carry social license, and that's for communities,
iwi, and the whole country as a whole.
And so Mercury's long-term aspiration is called Tuatara, which is to be
the partner of choice, and that shapes how we develop and how we operate, and
also shapes our approach to these bills.
So with that in mind, there are three core points that we've made throughout
our submission. The first one is to front load the effort
on upstream settings to ensure the downstream decisions are
reliable, high quality and predictable.
The second is to make environmental limits and biodiversity setting
workable in practice, especially for complex catchments and
existing operations. And the third is to keep consents and
per- permits proportionate, disciplined and durable, as that will
strengthen the social license that I just spoke to, spoke about.
This is important to Mercury because we're currently investing at scale, and our
half year results that we presented just a few weeks ago confirmed
three key things that we're currently doing.
The first is a five hundred and ninety million dollar hydro enhancement program
that's on the Waikato River that will provide an additional power for eight
thousand homes a year and extend the life stations for many
decades. The second is the Ngātamareki fifth-year
thermal unit, which we are officially opening tomorrow, and it started
generating power in January. That produces enough power for about
fifty thousand homes a year. Uh, and this third point
is the two wind farms that we've currently got under construction.
One in the far south, in Southland, and one in
Northland. Both of those will produce enough power for another eighty-seven
thousand homes. So those are pretty significant, three projects
alongside a, a large development pipeline we have, which has
about seventeen terawatt-hours of energy in it.
Just to put that into context, that would be enough to provide another two million
homes if we built all that. So we're not, we're not short of opportunity.
So these long-lived infrastructure investments, they sit right at the intersection
of the energy trilemma, to make sure we're keeping supply secure,
prices affordable, and doing it sustainably.
We have a strategic framework in Mercury, which is called Better
Today, Building Tomorrow and Brighter Together, and this reform
translates really well into that.
We need to run the existing assets safely and responsibly.
We need to build new g- new generation for New Zealand and do it in a way that
communities and iwi can benefit from it in the long term.
So just coming back, I'll touch on those three core messages I spoke to.
Uh, the first one was around getting the upstream settings right to unlock the
speed and the quality of the downstream.
And the message there is that the bill's promise of a more permissive, faster
system really does depend on the upstream quality of the national
instruments and the regional plans.
So we support a plan-led system where we have a clear hierarchy
of strong national direction, including more standardization to
improve consistency. We also support the planning bill's clearer
delineation of what effects are in scope and out of sco- scope because
that improves predictability. Two practical asks from our
submission. First, when the national instruments are developed or
reviewed, they require strong technical input.
Uh, that includes from technical practitioners, so that the standards are
workable and, and implementable.
Second, ensure meaningful early engagement with iwi authorities
on the first suite of instruments.
That's really critical to ensure that it's durable and it reduces the conflict that
may or may come later. And then on spatial planning, we support
identifying broad opportunities and constraints for renewable
generation, but we caution against overly prescriptive zoning
that could lock, site allocation and become
anti-competitive. The system needs to enable projects that are technically and
commercially feasible while still managing the real environmental
constraints. So our bottom line there is if we want better decisions downstream,
we have to work on the upstream, providing evidence-based national
instruments, implementable plan and partnership baked in right from the
start. Second message, core message was around environmental
limits that must lift outcomes without destabilizing existing
renewable generation. And so our message there is about
environmental limits and how they land in the real world.
Mercury supports environmental limits that deliver strong environmental
outcomes, but limits only work if they come from cl--
but limits that only work if they come from clear
responsibilities, clear compliance pathways, and a fair approach to
allocation and catchments that have multiple contributors.
So in our submission, we're very direct in that place.
We-- The framework needs to clearly address how limits interact with each
other, especially with lawful operations currently underway, including
how any allocation will actually operate.
Otherwise, we'll create the uncertainty of disputes and you risk constraining
generation that currently exists in ways that undermine energy
security. So for Mercury, hydro is a really clear example.
If you're using the Waikato hydro scheme, if any environmental limit
is exceeded at a catchment scale-The system must be clear on how
responsibility is allocated and what actions are required so
operators aren't subject to unpredictable operational constraints.
So we strongly support adaptive management being explicitly embedded as a
term, as a tool inside both the bills.
It matters most where knowledge evolves over time, and geothermal's another really
good example. At Ngātamareke, that, the field I just spoke to
before, field management relies on monitoring the key reservoir
parameters to making sure that it's sustainable over time, and you're
continually injec- in- adjusting the amount of fluid that you inject back
into the field and how much production you're taking out of the field to manage the
pressure and temperature of the reservoir.
And so that approach protects the resource and the environment without forcing
constant consent variations. So our point here is that
strong outcomes require clarity and implementability, not
just ambition, and the framework should drive improvement over time
without accidentally eroding the existing renewable
generation. Our final message, the, the third message, is
about keeping delivery settings disciplined and proportionate, and
avoiding creating any new delay, rights.
So this is really about delivery and about having confidence in the system.
Um, there's three key points in our submission.
Firstly, appeal rights should be confirmed to matters r- raised in
submissions. Allowing new matters on appeal just increases the
uncertainty and cost and creates incentives for tactical behavior.
Next bullet point, procedural review rights.
They need to be tight framed and short time limits, otherwise you create
new delay rights for people who are not directly affected.
And thirdly, permitted pathways matter.
Uh, mandatory registration of all permitted activities would add an ad-
administrative burden with little environmental gain in our view.
It would make routine maintenance and minor upgrades really hard.
We also support time-certain pathways for specified energy
activities, but only if extension and procedural review settings are tightly
framed so the pathways stay credible.
And a point particularly relevant to renewables is that we support retaining the
planning bill's exclusion of general landscape effects, visual amenity,
and views from private property. Let me bring that to life with, our
Turitea wind farm and our board of inquiry process.
Our final consented layout was for 60 turbines.
Uh, that was a significant reduction for what was applied for, which was over 120
turbines. Landscape and visual effects were the contributing factors to that
outcome. So we accepted at the time that outcome and the project was
built. It shows that we do make real changes in respect to landscape
values and community concerns, but it also shows why the system needs
clear boundaries and nationally consistent scope rules.
So in closing, Mercury supports the direction of these reforms.
We think the bills can materially improve consistency, reduce process
cost, and strengthen long-term confidence, provided the guardrails
adopt, are adopted so we can get strong environmental outcomes with practical
delivery. So three points to recap on, put the effort into
upstream settings so downstream decisions are high quality and predictable,
make environmental limits and biovesil- biodiversity settings
workable in practice, especially for complex catchments and
existing operations, and keep consents and permits
proportionate, disciplined, and durable because this will strengthen the social
license and enables delivery. So thank you very much.
Uh, Christine and myself are very happy to take your questions.

sorry, I've got... Hang on, I've got a niggle here.

we, we do believe environmental limits are necessary.

then those need to be spelt out at the start so that
we don't find we're all inadvertently having to put constraints on the way
we operate because somebody else is behaving within
the catchment effectively.
Mm.
Okay. No, thank you. I mean, it's quite a weighty part in your submission

Look, we'll try you again, Scott. We'll see if your audio's working.
Thank you. Um, and thanks Stu and Christina.
Um-
I apologise for the muck up earlier.
Uh, look, I really get your point about, visual,
visual effects and having had a wind farm declined
on the only thing it could be declined on, which was vis- visual

But, look, to the, the point that you've
said that national- consistent national direction is key,
really I've got a couple of questions there.
Would a bipartisan
national energy strategy provide that, that greater certainty that,
that you think the sector needs? That's, that's
one. And then
a second question, spatial planning you've talked about from page nine
onwards, are you comfortable that local
government has all the resources and expertise available to lead

well, then I'll, I'll get, I'll then I've got a, one follow-up
question.
Sure. I, I can touch on the, the first question was on basically strategy,

make a big difference, across industry,
certainly for Mercury, but also more broadly because it, what it does is it sets

So w- we've certainly been a big advocate of that and would love to see that,

So yep, we'd very much like to see that, and it would make a big difference.
Um, spatial planning, Christina, you could probably-
Um,
spatial planning I think for renewables particularly works brilliantly
for existing sites. Um, where we tread
into unknown territory is how
the competitive nature of our s- our business, so the electricity
sector is a competitive market-based system.
If we start prescribing where renewable
electricity can go or needs to go, like development areas and
things like that, we become a lot more constrained.
Um, one, I don't think spatial planning will be able to move as
quickly as the market can and as we can, and so
therefore there will be missed opportunities if we can't
mobilize to go where the power needs to go, where that
renewable generation needs to be built.
Whether or not regional councils have that capability or councils have that
capability, I think with existing renewables,
absolutely, because they're already dealing with it, and I think as long as we're
at the table and having that conversation with them as well,
then yes, I think we can achieve good things.
But I do think, there needs to be stronger guardrails around
how, competition and renewables are planned for in
the, in a spatial planning context.
Thank you. And have I got one sup, if I may?
Yeah, one sup. Go for it, Scott.
Thank you. And, I think w- what we've seen

local area energy planning could, could

Potentially, yeah, potentially.

discussions with the regional, Waikato Regional Council.
So yeah, I do think potentially because you're right, those people are already at
the table having the conversation about
renew- renewables and electricity within their region,
understanding where industry is that needs to d- decarbonize,
understanding where growth is going to occur.
All of those things help us when it comes to knowing where to
invest.
Thank you.
Thank you. Look, I might just jump in quickly with another question because
obviously you spoke about the huge potential that there is within
the energy sector to bring on renewable energy, and you gave some

Have you done any kind of numbers, around that in terms
of improving the resource management system to be able to get this
renewable energy up and running faster?
Haven't, haven't got any specific dollar numbers that I can, I can point to, but
it'll be in the many millions of dollars for us.
Um, I look at the, the time it takes us from a, a
twinkle in an eye to actually producing electricity, so from a
spark in the brain to a spark on the grid.
Um, at, at the moment that could be four or five years at best.
Um, for some of our projects it could be seven or eight years.
Uh, and, and at best it, it, it can probably be a couple of years.
So, that sort of time in between going from eight to two years makes a
significant difference. So now resource...
Uh, consenting is one aspect. There are obviously a number of other things that,

about, you know, four, five years, in a project which
we're typically spending, you know, half a billion to a billion dollars worth
that can add significant cost.
And does that also, you know, cause concern around certainty, I suppose, of
investment as well?
Yeah, yeah, very much so. So, w-we have a suite of
projects that we've got on our planning board, and we're trying to progress
them all at the right pace based on how good the projects are, but consenting comes
into that as well. So, potentially what it does is it means that the amount of
money we're trying to investing, not ourselves, but it'll be international
investment as well, it will absolutely be affected by how fast
those projects can be consented or consented at all.
Because for you, for us to be putting money at the early phase-
Mm

Or you focus on the projects that might not be the best for New
Zealand's electricity grid but might be the most consentable, and that
ultimately is not, not the best thing for, for New Zealand as a whole.
Mm. Now, look, we're gonna have to leave it there, Stu.
We've met time today. Uh, we appreciate, both of you
coming on and your very weighty, thorough submission with a lot of
considerations and, and lots of feedback there. So we appreciate that.
Great. Thanks for the opportunity.

So moving on to our next submission today, we have the New Zealand
Conservation Authority.
And we can see you there, Edward,
Wendy, and Paul. There we
go. We have you all with us. Welcome to the select committee this
afternoon. You have 20 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to
you to, run us through the main points in your submission and then of course
open for questions.
Tena koe Chair mana me nga member o te komiti kaiwhirowhiri.
Tena ra koutou. Ko Edward Ellison toku ikoa.
I am the chairperson of the New Zealand Conservation
Authority. Also present online, as you have mentioned, is Wendy
Nelson, deputy chairperson of the authority, and
authority member Paul Nursts. We will each present separate
components of our submission, starting with
myself. The New Zealand Conservation Authority is here to
speak to the proposed Clause 128
of the National Environmental Bill.
The authority was established under the Conservation Act
as an independent statutory body with a range of
functions, primarily acting as an independent
conservation advisor to the Minister and the
Director-General of Conservation.
The authority has a role as an objective advocate on matters of
national significance
and interest in the conservation arena.
Clause 128 of the NEB creates a new
avenue for wildlife permits to be obtained as part of
a natural resource permit,
and this has been ref-framed, as I quote here,
"to reduce the burden on developers and other operators,
we have added, have included a provision in the
NEB that will enable permit authorities
to authorize activities that would otherwise be unlawful under the
Wildlife Act." The stated intent
s- is also to streamline approvals by
removing the need to seek permissions from permit
authorities in the Department of Conservation for the
same activities. However, the bill does not say
how wildlife appro-approvals will be
determined, other than as part of a natural
resource permit. So the decision-making
requirements in Clause 154 to
167 of the bill that apply generally to natural
resource permits would also apply to wildlife
approval aspects of a permit. This means
decisions will be made by the, the permit authority
rather than by the Minister of Conservation
or their delegate. This is a very significant change
that lessens wildlife protections, because the NEB
does not contain the same protective purpose as the
Wildlife Act or a similar treaty
provision. Our submission traverses three
aspects of the clause. One, consequences
for the protection and management of wildlife.
Two, Te Tiriti. And three, underlying
justification for the proposed, proposed change.
I now pass on to Wendy Nelson.
Ko Wendy Nelson taku ingoa. Um, as well as being a member of the
authority, in my day job, I'm a biodiversity scientist and
botanist, and I'm s- addressing the consequences
of the proposed changes for protection and management of
wildlife. The bill does not contain a
wildlife-focused goal, and there's no reference to incorporation of
wildlife permit considerations into the national direction, into
spatial plans and national environment plans.
New Zealand has a really high proportion of threatened and at-risk indigenous
species for which we have national and international
responsibilities. The Wildlife Act protects all native
birds, native reptiles, frogs, bats, some land and
marine invertebrates, and nine marine fish.
It's unlawful to catch or kill protected species unless authorized by the
Director-General of Conservation via a wildlife permit.
And while resource consents may set broad conditions to manage the effects
of activity on indigenous biodiversity, wildlife
permits set much more specific technical requirements and often
specify how are mitigation activities to be undertaken
to appropriately protect wildlife.
It's not clear from Clause 128 if there will be one
authority issuing permits for a location or multiple
agencies, and this leads to the possibility of applicants shopping around,
leading to the processes for permits becoming really muddied and unclear,
both for those seeking and those administering
permits.Regional councils and territorial
authorities don't have wildlife protection functions, and as
such, they don't have the staff with the type of expertise required to
assess wildlife permits, unlike the Department of Conservation.
So there's a real risk that the deficits in knowledge and expertise are
not recognized by councils and decisions are not being made on the
best data and information currently available.
As noted in the Ministry for the Environment briefing paper, placing more
responsibility onto councils to set conditions for wildlife
protection will have major flow-on effects for the councils and also for the
department. Costs for these additional requirements
will be passed on to these local authorities with no apparent additional resource
intended to be allocated. The proposed
Clause one two eight will lead to a loss of coherence
in the protection responsibility for wildlife and will make
protection of threatened species more complex and less
efficient. There's no recognition of the
need for national oversight of threatened species currently protected by the
Wildlife Act. And under the proposed,
one two eight, there's no obligations for reporting or
coordinating information about the decisions and monitoring the
outcomes for New Zealand wildlife.
These are key roles of the Department of Conservation.
Data on wildlife needs to be held in a central and
coordinated system for national benefit and for consistent
decision-making. Now, the Department of Conservation has developed a
framework recently to enable decision-making about species, habitats, and
ecosystems. It's called BioInvest, and this system is enabling
transparency about decisions for priorities and
expenditure, and it's enabling the balancing and weighing of risks and
benefits for biodiversity of specific actions and investments.
There's a complete absence of policy framework to guide how wildlife
protection and decisions will be delivered by this bill.
And there's also been no consultation with the wider New Zealand community on the
proposed changes affecting wildlife.
So in the view of, Conservation Authority, Clause one two eight will lead to
inconsistent decision-making, a lack of clarity for applicants and for
those assessing permits, and loss of protection for
wildlife. I'll hand back now to Edward.
Oh, Edward, you just need to pop your audio on there.
Thank you for the reminder.
Yeah.
Uh, look, we've, we've observed through the reform process the Crown
has consistently failed to engage appropriately with
iwi. The rushed timeframe has cost the Crown the
opportunity to engage meaningfully with iwi on these
significant policy proposals. We note
that the e-expert advisory group recommended that the
bill should retain the Section eight of the RMA, which

including taonga species, and in some instances
requires the Crown to have particular regard to the views
of iwi. As currently drafted, the NEP
is not clear how wildlife approvals will uphold, for

Treaty settlements. I will now pass on to
Paul Norris.
Uh, Kia ora. Paul Norris
from, from, Relienzed, but

justification for the proposed changes.
Are consents for wildlife permits actually constraining developers?
The d-data show that Department of Conservation currently gets

applications. A very small proportion of
projects that need a resource consent also get a
wildlife permit. In twenty twenty-two, twenty-three,
thirty-one thousand eight hundred and two resource consents were

In this same period, four hundred and sixty-four wildlife permits

activities.
Over the last five years, only one business has raised concerns with
ministers about duplication between resource consent
and wildlife permits through official
channels. In the
past-- In the recent past, there has been concerns about the

process permit applications. However, in the
past twenty-four months, the Department of Conservation has done a great job
reducing the number of p- of outstanding concessions and
permits applications, and greatly speeding up the
processing of, and issuing of new
permits. In the last six months of twenty
twenty-four, fifty-two percent of Wildlife Act authority
permits were completed within seventy days.
In the same period of twenty twenty-five, seventy-five percent were
completed. It is the view of the New Zealand Conservation
AuthorityTo place clear time expectations on the
department to ensure efficient and timely turnarounds on
applications for the wildlife permits could be achieved.
We believe a 60-day working frame would be sufficient,
leaving the wildlife permit, permitting with the Department of
Conservation, the organization that holds the expertise for our
country.
This approach, with the Department of Conservation being
time-bound, would certainly lead to a better outcome for our
wildlife, and also keep the skill set within the
department that has exity- expertise to make
these decisions around wildlife permits.
In summary, the New Zealand Conservation Authority does not
support Clause 128 in the Natural Environment
Bill. We consider that it would be...
It would result a piecemeal decision-making.
The decisions are likely to be inconsistent across the country,
with decisions subject to the provisions of different natural environment
plans and concerns of the different regional
councils. Fragmented and inconsistent
management is not appropriate for threatened and at-risk
species which are categorized as such at a national
scale. The Wildlife Act most
certainly needs to be re- revised, but injecting this clause
to the Natural Environment Bill is not going to meet
either the stated intent of reducing the burden for developers
and other operators, and is certainly not going to result in
protection of our wildlife.
The Crown has consistently failed to engage appropriately with iwi.
The rushed timeframe has cost the Crown the opportunity to engage
meaningfully with iwi on this s- significant
policy proposals. The process to develop
the Natural Environment Bill and Planning Bill has to date not
reflected the partnership afforded within Te Tiriti, and does
not meet even reasonable expectations.
